title: POCUS-CARE Textbook
text:
Presentation
Point-of-Care Ultrasound for Conflict-Affected Regions (POCUS-CARE) is an educational program designed for healthcare professionals providing acute care in conflict-affected settings.
The program teaches Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS): ultrasound performed and interpreted by the treating clinician at the bedside to answer focused clinical questions and support immediate patient management.
The course is intended for physicians involved in emergency and critical care, including emergency physicians, intensivists, anesthesiologists, surgeons, pediatricians, nurses, paramedics, and other clinicians managing acutely ill or injured patients.
This handbook serves both as the reference text for the POCUS-CARE training program and as a practical resource for clinicians in daily practice. It combines ultrasound techniques, image interpretation, clinical integration, and evidence-based recommendations, with a particular focus on applications relevant to resource-limited and conflict-affected environments.

text:
This is a book created from markdown and executable code.
See Knuth (1984) for additional discussion of literate programming.

title: POCUS-CARE Textbook
text:
Course Principles
The training program is based on the following principles:

Action-oriented – Learning is organised around clinical questions that directly support diagnosis, triage, treatment, and referral decisions.
Context-adapted – While the educational framework is shared across countries, each national implementation is adapted to local epidemiology, healthcare systems, available resources, and national priorities.
Evidence-based – Educational content is developed by certified POCUS instructors and follows current international evidence and clinical guidelines. References are provided throughout the course.
Collaborative – National instructors lead the development, implementation, and continuous improvement of the program within their own countries.
Freely available – Educational materials are distributed under the licence described below, allowing non-commercial reuse and adaptation.

title: POCUS-CARE Textbook
text:
Learning Outcomes
After completing this program, participants will be able to:

Perform focused ultrasound examinations using a systematic scanning approach.
Identify common life-threatening conditions, including intra-abdominal bleeding, tamponnade and pneumothorax.
Use ultrasound findings to support the assessment of circulatory and respiratory failure.
Identify selected musculoskeletal conditions, including soft-tissue foreign bodies and long-bone diaphyseal fractures.
Identify deep vein thrombosis.
Perform ultrasound-guided vascular access.
Integrate ultrasound findings into clinical decision-making while recognising the limitations of POCUS and the potential for false-positive and false-negative findings.

title: POCUS-CARE Textbook
text:
In-Person Training Sessions
The POCUS-CARE program is currently implemented in:

Palestine (West Bank)
Lebanon in partnership with MedGlobal and Anera NGOs.

For information about upcoming courses or future implementations, please contact: pierre.catoire@mehad.fr

title: Learning objectives
text:
TipAfter completing this chapter, learners will be able to:




Describe the physical properties of ultrasound waves, including frequency, wavelength, propagation speed, and acoustic impedance.
Explain how ultrasound waves are generated, transmitted through tissues, reflected, scattered, attenuated, and received by the transducer.
Explain how ultrasound systems convert returning echoes into diagnostic images in B-mode and M-mode imaging.
Predict how changes in ultrasound frequency and tissue characteristics affect image penetration and spatial resolution.
Identify common ultrasound artifacts and explain the physical mechanisms responsible for their occurrence.
Interpret selected ultrasound images by distinguishing normal anatomical structures from common artifacts.
Describe the biological effects of diagnostic ultrasound and apply the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principle during ultrasound examinations.

title: What is an ultrasound?
text:
Nature of ultrasound waves
An ultrasound is a mechanical wave that propagates through matter as successive variations in pressure. Unlike electromagnetic waves such as light or X-rays, ultrasound cannot travel through a vacuum and requires a physical medium.
In medical imaging, ultrasound waves propagate through biological tissues by causing microscopic cycles of compression and rarefaction. Although these pressure variations are extremely small, they can travel through tissues and carry information back to the ultrasound probe.
An ultrasound wave is characterized by several properties, among which frequency, amplitude and wavelength are the most important.
Frequency corresponds to the number of oscillations occurring each second and is expressed in hertz (Hz). Diagnostic ultrasound typically uses frequencies between 2 and 15 MHz.
Amplitude corresponds to the magnitude of the pressure variation generated by the wave.
Wavelength is the distance travelled during one oscillation cycle. It is related to frequency by:
\[
\lambda = \frac{c}{f}
\]
where:

title: What is an ultrasound?
text:
\(\lambda\) = wavelength
\(c\) = speed of sound in the medium
\(f\) = frequency







TipKey Definitions




Frequency: number of oscillations per second.
Amplitude: magnitude of pressure variation.
Wavelength: distance covered during one oscillation cycle.
Ultrasound: sound waves with frequencies above 20 kHz. Medical ultrasound typically uses frequencies between 2 and 15 MHz.






Ultrasound frequency and amplitude








NoteAdvanced: Ultrasound Energy





The acoustic energy of an ultrasound wave (measured in Joules, \(J\)) is the mechanical energy transported through a medium by successive compressions and rarefactions.
In medical ultrasound, two related quantities are particularly useful:

Power (Watts, \(W\)): the amount of acoustic energy emitted per unit time.
Intensity (\(W/m^2\)): the acoustic power transmitted per unit area.

In practice, the user of an ultrasound device can influence several parameters related to acoustic energy:

Acoustic power (output power): increasing the acoustic power increases the amount of energy emitted per unit time.
Pulse duration: longer pulses contain more energy and therefore deliver more energy during each emission.
Pulse repetition frequency (PRF): increasing the PRF increases the number of emitted pulses per second and therefore increases the average power delivered to tissues.

Note that the ultrasound frequency does not directly determine the emitted energy. However, higher-frequency waves are attenuated more rapidly in tissues and therefore deposit their energy over a shorter distance.
Finally, gain is a signal amplification parameter applied to received echoes. It does not modify the ultrasound waves emitted by the probe and therefore has no effect on acoustic energy.

title: What is an ultrasound?
text:
Frequency, Penetration and Resolution
The frequency affects both resolution and penetration:

Low-frequency waves can travel deeper through tissues before becoming attenuated. They therefore provide better penetration and are used to image deep structures such as the heart or abdominal organs.
High-frequency waves are attenuated more rapidly. Their penetration is therefore limited. However, because their wavelength is shorter, they can distinguish smaller structures and provide superior spatial resolution.








Low Frequency
High Frequency




Better penetration
Better resolution


Lower resolution
Lower penetration


Use for superficial exam (skin, superficial vessels …)
Use for deep exam (abdomen, heart, brain …)









TipA useful analogy



A useful analogy is the music heard from a distant party. Deep bass sounds remain audible from far away, whereas high-pitched sounds disappear more quickly. Similarly, low-frequency ultrasound travels deeper into tissues, whereas high-frequency ultrasound provides finer anatomical detail.

title: What is an ultrasound?
text:
Emission, Propagation, Absorption and Scattering

Emission and the piezo-electric effect
Ultrasound waves are generated by the probe.
The probe contains multiple piezoelectric crystals capable of converting electrical energy into mechanical vibrations and mechanical vibrations into electrical signals. This phenomenon is known as the piezoelectric effect. The same crystals therefore act both as emitters and receivers.



Piezoelectric cells in an ultrasound probe




Propagation Through Biological Tissues
Once emitted, ultrasound waves propagate through tissues.
For imaging purposes, ultrasound systems assume an average propagation speed of:
\[
c = 1540\ \text{m/s}
\]
in soft tissues.


Attenuation, Absorption and Scattering
As the wave propagates, part of its energy is progressively lost. This phenomenon is called attenuation.
Attenuation results mainly from:

Absorption: Part of the mechanical energy is converted into heat.
Scattering: Small structures redirect ultrasound energy in multiple directions.



Reflection and transmission
Each medium (water, fat, bone, gas …) has its own acoustic impedance (noted \(Z\)), which describes how easily the ultrasound propagates.

Acoustic impedances of biological tissues, in MRayl (\(10^6 \cdot kg \cdot m^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}\))


Water
Fat
Bone
Gas




1.48
1.33
6-8
0.0004



As long as the medium is homogeneous, the impedance is constant and no reflection occurs. However, when the wave transitions from a medium to another (the surface between them is called the interface), the ultrasound wave separates between transmitted and reflected ultrasounds:

the transmitted ultrasound continues towards the new medium
the reflected ultrasound bounces back in the opposite direction

Note that because the energy is conserved, the sum of the energies of the transmitted and reflected ultrasound, and the energy between the ultrasound hitting the interface (called the “incident” ultrasound) is constant:

strong reflection implies weak transmission;
weak reflection implies strong transmission.

Differences in acoustic impedance determine the intensity of the reflection and transmission:

an interface between two media with large difference of impedance is highly reflective
an interface between two media with small difference of impedance is weakly reflective




Reflection and transmission with a weakly reflective interface (left) and a strongly reflective surface (right)








ImportantCommon Misconception



Many learners believe that bone is the strongest reflector of ultrasound.
Bone reflects a large proportion of ultrasound energy, but gas reflects even more. Actually, ultrasound is possible through bones (for example, transcranial doppler), but impossible through gas.
From an ultrasound perspective, gas behaves like a mirror. No structure can be seen if gas is present between the probe and the structure. This is why:

ultrasound gel is needed: without gel, a thin layer of air remains trapped between the probe and the skin.
ultrasound can see only the superficial layers of the bowel and the pleura
if pneumothorax is present, only the parietal pleura is visible, the visceral pleura is hidden by the gas layer of the pneumothorax









WarningKey points




An ultrasound is a mechanical wave, not an electromagnetic wave
it is characterised by its frequency and amplitude
the lower frequencies penetrate better but have poorer resolution
when hitting an interface, a part of the ultrasound is reflected and the remaining part is transmitted to the new medium
gas is highly reflective in human tissues

title: Image formation and modes
text:
The Pulse - Echo Principle
Diagnostic ultrasound relies on the pulse-echo principle.
The ultrasound system measures the time elapsed between emission and reception.
Because the speed of sound in soft tissues is assumed to be constant, the depth of the reflecting interface can be calculated:
\[
\text{Depth}
=
\frac{c \times t}{2}
\]
where:

title: Image formation and modes
text:
The probe emits a short pulse of ultrasound and then waits for returning echoes.
Ultrasounds are reflected when they hit interfaces
When a reflected ultrasound (“echo”) reaches the probe, it is converted into an electrical signal by the piezoelectric crystals.






\(c\) = speed of sound in tissue;
\(t\) = measured round-trip travel time.







NoteExample



Suppose an echo returns after:
\[
t = 130\ \mu s
\]
The depth of the reflecting interface is:
\[
\frac{1540 \times 130\times10^{-6}}{2}
=
0.10\ m
\]
The interface is therefore located approximately 10 cm from the probe.





The pulse-echo principle (AI generated)

title: Image formation and modes
text:
Ultrasound Modes
The mode describes how the ultrasound system displays the echoes received from tissues. Several imaging modes are available.

A-mode (Amplitude mode): the earliest ultrasound mode. Echoes are displayed as peaks whose height is proportional to echo intensity. It is now rarely used in clinical practice.
B-mode (Brightness mode or 2D mode): grayscale image representing a two-dimensional section of the tissues.
M-mode (Motion mode or TM): representation of tissue motion along a single ultrasound line over time.

Doppler imaging and three-dimensional ultrasound are not covered in this course.

B-Mode (2D Mode)
Each emitted ultrasound beam explores a single direction through the body.
Along this path, multiple interfaces reflect varying amounts of ultrasound energy.
For each echo received, the machine determines:

The depth of the interface from the travel time.
The brightness of the interface from the echo intensity.

By emitting multiple beams in adjacent directions and assembling the corresponding lines, the ultrasound system reconstructs a two-dimensional representation of the scanned tissues.
The resulting image is the standard grayscale image used for most ultrasound examinations.



B-mode (AI generated)




M-Mode
In M-mode, the ultrasound system repeatedly samples a single ultrasound line over time.
The image displays:

time on the horizontal axis;
depth on the vertical axis;
brightness according to echo intensity.

Structures that remain stationary appear as horizontal lines.
Structures moving toward or away from the probe change depth over time and generate oblique or curved traces.
Structures moving perpendicular to the ultrasound beam may intermittently enter and leave the sampled line, producing discontinuous traces.
M-mode is used mostly to measure variation of lengths over time, or to help identify subtle motion patterns.



M-mode (AI generated)








WarningKey points




The ultrasound systems translates received echoes into images, using the pulse echo principle
Each echo indicates an interface: the distance between the probe and the interface is estimated from the travel time of the echo
The B mode (or 2D) is a two dimensional slice view
The M mode (or TM) represents the movement of structurs along one dimension

title: Probes
text:
Different types of probes
Ultrasound probes vary depending on the intended use. In particular, two characteristics matters when choosing a probe:

title: Probes
text:
the shape of the probe surface
the frequency range: although the frequency of the probe can vary, the size of the piezoelectric crystals in the probe impose a range of frequencies


Probe surface shape
The shape of the probe surface influences the geometry of the scanned area:

a curved surface will naturally emit ultrasounds in a fan-shaped examination area. Those probes are called curvilinear
A wide flat surface typically produces a rectangular field of view and is characteristic of linear probes.



Array phasing
In some contexts, a wide, fan-shaped scanning area is required but anatomical obstacles make the use of probes with wide contact surface difficult. This is the case for thoracic ultrasound (because of the ribs) or for transcranial ultrasound (because of the skull).
However, it is possible to emit ultrasound in oblique directions from a flat surface by “bending” the emission direction. This relies on the arrray phasing principle. Here, a small, rectangular flat surface is used (called sectorial). Array phasing is used to obtain a fan-shaped scanning area, and therefore those probes are also sometimes called “phased array”1.



Probes and scanning areas (AI generated)








NoteAdvanced: Array phasing principle





To emit an ultrasound pulse, the probe activates simultaneously an adjacent group of piezoelectric cells, called an array.
However, by delaying the time of emission between the different cells of the array, the wavefront is deviated from the perpendicular axis. Using this effect is called “array phasing” and allows to emit in oblique directions from a flat surface.



Array phasing (AI generated)

title: Probes
text:
Available probes
To fit the different needs of medical uses, manufacturers have designed probes whose characteristics make them use in context adapted. Three main types of probes are available: linear, curvilinear and sectorial:

Characteristics and usage of probes








Characteristic
Linear
Curvilinear
Sectorial




Frequency range
5-15 MHz (up to 18-20 MHz for some probes)
2-5 MHz
1-5 MHz


Typical depth
4-8 cm
20-30 cm
20-30 cm


Surface shape
Flat
Curved
Flat


Contact surface
Large
Large
Small


Scanning area shape
Rectangular
Fan-shaped
(because of the curved surface)
Fan-shaped
(because of the use of array phasing)


Uses
Superficial exams (vessels, nerves, bones, pleura …)
Abdomen
Obstetrics
Heart
Lungs (deep exam)
Transcranial doppler

title: Probes
text:
Which probe for which usage?
When available, the probe the most suited should be chosen. However, in constrained settings not all the probes are always available. The following table summarises the relevance of each probe for each use:

Probe uses. ✓: best choice. ◐: acceptable choice. ✗: not adapted/recommended.


Usage
Linear
Curvilinear
Sectorial




Superficial vessels
✓
✗
✗


Deep vessels (aorta, …)
✗
✓
◐


Superficial nerves
✓
✗
✗


Superficial foreign bodies
✓
✗
✗


Abdomen
✗
✓
◐


Heart
◐
✗
✓

✓
◐
◐

◐
◐
✓


Lungs (costodiaphragmatic space)
✗
✓
✓


Intracranial
✗
✗
✓

✓
✗
✗









WarningKey points




The linear probe is used for examination of surface structures: skin, superficial bones, vessels, nerves …
The curvilinear probe is used for examination of deep structures without obstacles: abdomen, obstetrics …
The sectorial (or phased array) probe is used for examination of deep structures with obstacles: heart, brain …

title: Probes
text: Note however that array phasing is not a characteristic of the probe itself, and that it may be used with linear or curvilinear probes as well.↩︎

title: Artifacts
text:
A-lines (or reverberation artifacts)
Artifacts are defined as images which do not correspond to anatomical structures. They appear when the ultrasound system misinterprets echoes, resulting in inappropriate image. The user must be aware of the potential presence of artifacts to avoid confusion when interpreting.
Importantly, artifacts may also be used as interpretable features: their presence may indicate specific tissue configurations which can be interpreted alongside anatomical findings. This is particularly the case for lung ultrasound, where the intense reflection caused by air induces different kinds of artifacts.
When the ultrasound wave hits a highly reflective surface perpendicular to the emission axis, almost all of the ultrasound wave is reflected directly to the probe. This reflected echo may be strong enough to bounce against the probe itself, and being reflected again against the reflective surface.
From the perspective of the ultrasound system, this second echo is received at twice the travel time of the first ultrasound, and is interpreted as a second reflective surface, at a distance to the probe which is twice the distance of the initial one. This manifest as a horizontal bright line.
A-lines are typically encountered against pleura or long bones. They have no specific significance and must be ignored.

title: Artifacts
text: A-lines (from radiopedia.org)

title: Artifacts
text:
B-lines (or comet tails)
B-lines occur when the ultrasound wave is “trapped” in closely spaced reflective surfaces. Multiple echoes with short delays will exit the space with reflective surfaces, and reach the probe with a small delay, which are represented as a vertical bright line.
B-lines are typically present in water-gas interfaces, where small gas bubbles act as “traps”. They are hence present on the visceral pleura, stomach, and bowels. In lungs, they suggest interstitial edema (either inflammatory or hydrostatics) when they are numerous.



B-lines (from radiopedia.org)

title: Artifacts
text:
Acoustic Shadowing
Acoustic shadowing results from high reflective surfaces. When an incident ultrasound is strongly reflected, the transmitted ultrasound is weak and unable to produce deeper echoes. This manifests as a dark shadow beyond the highly reflective surface. This occurs typically after soft, highly reflective structures such as bones or stones.



Acoustic shadowing (from radiopedia.org)

title: Artifacts
text:
Posterior Acoustic Enhancment
Opposite with acoustic shadowing, anechogenic structures cause the ultrasounds to preserve their energy. When hitting the posterior wall of those structures, the incident ultrasound has more energy and the reflection is more intense, represented as a brighter image.
This artifact is encountered with anechogenic or hypoechogenic structures such as bladder, gallbladder, or cysts.



Posterior Acoustic Enhancment (from radiopedia.org)

title: Artifacts
text:
Edge shadowing artifact
Edge shadowing occurs when ultrasound waves encounter the curved edge of a structure. At these interfaces, part of the ultrasound beam is refracted away from its original path.
Because less ultrasound energy reaches the tissues located behind the edge, fewer echoes return to the probe. The ultrasound system therefore displays a dark shadow extending posteriorly from the margins of the structure.
Edge shadowing artifact appears mainly with vessels, gallbladder, or cysts.



Edge shadowing (from European Society of Radiology)

title: Artifacts
text:
Mirror artifact
Highly reflective structures may act as a mirror. The ultrasound system assumes that the echoes received after reflection actually come from a “shadow structure” beyond the reflective surface, producing a mirror image accross a bright reflective surface. This pattern typically appears with the diaphragm.



Mirror artifact (from radiopedia.org)








WarningKey Points




Artifacts are images superimposed over the anatomical structures caused by misinterpretation of echoes by the ultrasound system
A-lines appear when a highly reflective structure is perpendicular to the ultrasound beam
B-lines appear when the ultrasound wave reaches a tight network of reflective structures, such as water/air transition surfaces
Acoustic shadowing appears when a highly reflective structure deflects an important part of the ultrasound energy
Posterior enhancment occurs after an anechogenic or hypoechogenic structure
Edge shadowing occurs with round-shaped hypoechogenic structures
Mirror artifact occurs with oblique highly reflective structures, typically the diaphragm

title: Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound
text:
Thermal Effects
Diagnostic ultrasound is considered one of the safest imaging modalities available. Unlike X-rays and CT scans, ultrasound does not use ionizing radiation and therefore does not expose patients to radiation-related risks.
However, ultrasound waves carry energy and interact with biological tissues. Although no harmful effects have been demonstrated for routine diagnostic ultrasound performed according to current recommendations, ultrasound is theoretically not entirely free of biological effects.
As ultrasound propagates through tissues, part of its energy is absorbed and converted into heat.
This phenomenon is known as the thermal effect.
Under normal imaging conditions, the resulting temperature increase is very small and unlikely to cause tissue injury. However, heating may become more significant when:
Thermal effects are of particular concern during fetal imaging because developing tissues may be more sensitive to temperature changes.
crumbs: 6  Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound

title: Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound
crumbs: 6  Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound

title: Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound
text:
Mechanical Effects
Ultrasound waves also produce mechanical forces within tissues.
In the presence of microscopic gas bubbles, these forces may cause the bubbles to oscillate, a phenomenon known as cavitation.
Although cavitation has been demonstrated experimentally, it has not been shown to cause harm during routine diagnostic ultrasound examinations performed according to current safety recommendations.
crumbs: 6  Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound

title: Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound
text:
Thermal Index and Mechanical Index
Modern ultrasound systems continuously estimate the potential thermal and mechanical effects of ultrasound and display two safety indicators:

Thermal Index (TI): an estimate of the potential tissue heating.
Mechanical Index (MI): an estimate of the likelihood of mechanical effects such as cavitation.

Higher values indicate a greater potential for biological effects.






NoteAdvanced: Interpreting TI and MI





The Thermal Index (TI) is an estimate of the maximum temperature increase that could occur under specific conditions.
The Mechanical Index (MI) reflects the relationship between the peak negative pressure of the ultrasound wave and its frequency. Lower frequencies generally produce higher MI values for the same acoustic pressure.
crumbs: 6  Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound

title: Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound
text:
The ALARA Principle
The cornerstone of ultrasound safety is the ALARA principle:
As Low As Reasonably Achievable
This principle states that ultrasound exposure should be kept as low as reasonably achievable while obtaining the information required for clinical decision-making.
In practice, this means:

using the lowest acoustic output compatible with adequate image quality,
limiting examination time to what is clinically necessary,
avoiding prolonged scanning of a single region without justification,
paying particular attention during fetal examinations.
crumbs: 6  Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound

title: Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound
section: Recommended ranges of TI and MI
text:
Recommended ranges of TI and MI
The TI maximal values recommended by the American Institute of Ultrasound in Medicine for fetal exam are as follows:



Thermal Index (TI)
Maximum recommended dwell time




≤ 0.7
No time limit (ALARA)


> 0.7 – 1.0
< 60 min


> 1.0 – 1.5
< 30 min


> 1.5 – 2.0
< 15 min


> 2.0 – 2.5
< 4 min


> 2.5 – 3.0
< 1 min


> 3.0
Not recommended



For MI, the recommended ranges are:

for ophtalmic ultrasound: MI < 0.23
for routine use: MI < 1.9, ALARA







WarningKey Points




Ultrasound has theoretical mechanical and thermal side effects although these effects have not been observed in practice
Ultrasound does not have chemical, electromagnetic or radiation side effects
Ultrasound must be used respecting the “As Low as Reasonably Achievable” (ALARA) principle
Thermal effects are dependent of the exam duration, which must be limited particularly for fetal exam. It is estimated with the Thermal Index (TI)
Mechanical effects are independent from the exam duration. It is estimated with the Mechanical Index (MI) and must be limited particularly for eye exam
crumbs: 6  Safety and Adverse Effects of Ultrasound

title: Learning Objectives
text:
TipAfter completing this chapter, the learner will be able to:




Position themself and the patient adequately
Use maneuvers to optimise image quality
Set the essential device settings appropriately: depth, width, gain, Time Gain Compensation (TGC), focal distance
Position the probe in order to obtain the best view using elementary movements: sliding, rocking, rotation

title: Positioning the patient
text:
Patient position influence the position of organs, and in particular gases. Depending on the target, positioning the patient or asking the patient some maneuvers (inspiration, expiration, Valsalva …)






WarningCareful with manipulation!



Always consider whether or not the patient can be safely manipulated before proceeding:

patients with clinical suspicion of spine injury should preserve their spinal alignment
patients with risk of increased intracranial pressure should not perform the Valsalva maneuver



Here are some common maneuvers to consider:

To improve visualisation of hepatorenal space of splenorenal space, position the patient lying on the opposite side
To better visualise kidneys or spleen, ask the patient to inhale deeply and hold their breath
To improve visualisation of the gallbladder, position the patient in lateral position, lying on his left side
To remove the gases between the probe and the abdominal vessels (aorta, vena cava), press gently the probe to the back of the patient
To improve subcostal cardiac view, ask the patient to inspire deeply and hold their breath
To improve visualisation of jugular vein, ask the patient to perform a Valsalva maneuver

Also, the position of the examiner is important to ensure confortable exam. The examiner is usually placed on the right side of the patient for cardiac exam, holding the probe with his/her right hand (even for left handers).

title: Device settings
text:
The number of parameters available in modern ultrasound devices can be intimidating. However, most of the image quality actually depends on a few key settings. In the following section, we describe only the essential parameters and invite the reader to see his/her device manual for advanced settings.

Depth and width
The scanning frame should be deep enough and wide enough to visualise all relevant structures. However, the depth and width of the frame affect the number of frames per second (FPS):

Deepening the frame requires for the ultrasound device to “wait” for the more distant structures to emit echo, and therefore this reduces the PRF, implying more time to compose a full image
Widening the frame requires to emit more pulses to scan the whole frame, implying more time to compose a full image

Also, a too deep or wide frame results in a “zoom out” effect which makes important structures smaller.
[XXX Image]
Therefore, the frame should not be deeper nor wider than what is needed.


Gain
The gain define the brightness of the image. If must be set at a medium value sufficient to prevent overexposure or underexposure.
[XXX image]


Time Gain Compensation (TGC)
Time Gain Compensation (TGC) allows for adjusting the gain at a specific depth. This is helpful especially when structure is highly hyperechogenic, resulting in underexposing structures beyond it, or conversely if a structure is highly hypoechogenic, resulting in overexposing structures beyond it.
TGC should always be positioned in neutral position first when starting the exam, and be adjusted carefully, only if one depth is significantly too bright or too dark.
[XXX image]


Focal distance
Similarly to cameras, ultrasound devices can optimise the resolution at a specific distance (implying a degradation of resolution for structures closer and more distant). The focal distance must be set at the distance of the structure of most interest.

title: Orientation
text:
Understanding image orientation
Correct probe orientation is essential for obtaining and interpreting ultrasound images. The examiner must position and orient the probe so that the ultrasound beam passes through the structures of interest.
Because patient anatomy varies, there is no single correct probe position. Instead, the examiner continuously adjusts the probe while maintaining an accurate understanding of the orientation of the image displayed on the screen.
Two skills are therefore fundamental:
An ultrasound image is a cross-sectional view of the body. Each side of the image corresponds to a specific anatomical direction.
To correctly interpret the image, the examiner must always know which direction is represented by:
This process is similar to interpreting CT images: every side of the image corresponds to one of the anatomical directions (left, right, anterior, posterior, cranial or caudal).

title: Orientation
text:
the top of the screen;
the bottom of the screen;
the left side of the screen;
the right side of the screen.



The probe marker
Every ultrasound probe has a marker located on one side of its tip. This marker is represented on the ultrasound screen by a small symbol (usually a dot or a triangle).
The marker establishes which side of the probe corresponds to one side of the displayed image.
XXX Image


The four sides of the ultrasound image
Regardless of the examination being performed, every ultrasound image has four borders:

Ultrasound entry: where the ultrasound beam enters the body.
Ultrasound exit: the deepest part of the displayed image.
Marker side: the side corresponding to the probe marker.
Opposite side: the side opposite the probe marker.

XXX Image
The examiner must identify these four borders before interpreting the anatomy.






WarningCaution!



For most of the exams, the ultrasound entry is at the top of the screen, and the mark sign is on the left side of the screen. This is in particular the case for abdomen and vascular exam. However, some other settings

cardiac exam: the marker side is usually on the right side of the screen
endocavitary gynecologic exam: the origin of ultrasounds is usually on the bottom side of the screen




Cardiac ultrasound. Note that the mark is on the right side of the screen





Endocavitary gynecological exam. Note that the ultrasound entry is on the bottom of the screen, and the mark is on the right side.

title: Orientation
Orientation
text:
Examples

Example 1: Transversal viewExample 2: Sagittal viewExample 3: Frontal view

title: Probe Movements
text:
Longitudinal sliding
Obtaining a high-quality ultrasound image requires continuous adjustment of the probe. Small changes in its position, orientation, or pressure can considerably improve the visualization of anatomical structures.
Each ultrasound image represents a two-dimensional imaging plane through the body. Probe movements either preserve this imaging plane while changing the viewing angle, move it to a neighbouring anatomical section, or rotate it.
Six fundamental probe movements are commonly used (Figure XX).
Longitudinal sliding consists of translating the probe along its long axis while maintaining the same orientation and constant contact with the skin.
This movement preserves the imaging plane and is used to follow anatomical structures within the same section or to centre a structure on the screen.

title: Probe Movements
text: Long-axis sliding (AI generated)

title: Probe Movements
text:
Long-axis tilting
Long-axis tilting consists of pivoting the probe around its long axis while maintaining skin contact.
This changes the angle of insonation without moving the imaging plane to another anatomical section. It is useful for bringing an entire structure into view or obtaining a more perpendicular incidence of the ultrasound beam.



Long-axis tilting (AI generated)

title: Probe Movements
text:
Lateral sliding
Lateral sliding consists of translating the probe perpendicular to its long axis while keeping the probe parallel to the skin.
Unlike longitudinal sliding, this movement shifts the imaging plane to a neighbouring anatomical section while preserving its orientation. It is used to explore adjacent structures.



Short-axis sliding (AI generated)

title: Probe Movements
text:
Short-axis tilting
Short-axis tilting consists of pivoting the probe around its short axis.
This tilts the imaging plane, obtaining an oblique view.



Short-axis tilting (AI generated)

title: Probe Movements
text:
Rotation
Rotation consists of turning the probe around its central axis while maintaining skin contact. This movement is commonly used to switch between transverse and longitudinal views of a structure, such as a vessel or tendon.



Rotation (AI generated)

title: Probe Movements
text:
Compression
Compression consists of applying gentle pressure with the probe against the patient’s skin. It may be useful to remove the abdominal gases, or to test pulsatility and compressibility of vessels.

title: Learning Objectives
text:
TipLearning objectives



After completing this chapter, learners will be able to:

Explain the indications and limitations of the FAST examination.
Obtain all standard FAST views.
Recognize normal anatomy in each view.
Identify free intraperitoneal and pericardial fluid.
Interpret FAST findings according to the clinical context.
Recognize common pitfalls and avoid frequent mistakes.

title: Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol
text:
Why POCUS for Trauma Patients?
Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) has become an essential component of emergency and critical care assessment of abdominal complaints. In addition to trauma assessment, abdominal POCUS is widely used to evaluate non-traumatic conditions such as abdominal aortic aneurysm, biliary disease, hydronephrosis, urinary obstruction, nephrolithiasis, and cholecystitis (1).
Among these applications, trauma assessment played a pivotal role in the development of emergency POCUS. The seminal publication by Rozycki et al. (2) described the Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma (FAST) protocol, a standardized bedside examination performed by trauma surgeons to rapidly identify:
Importantly, the FAST examination was not designed to diagnose specific organ injuries. Instead, its objective was to rapidly identify life-threatening internal bleeding requiring immediate intervention.
The original study reported a sensitivity of 79.0% and a specificity of 95.6% for detecting free intraperitoneal fluid after only 8 hours of operator training. The authors proposed FAST for patients presenting with:
FAST was incorporated into the 6th edition of the Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) guidelines in 1996 (3). Since then, improvements in trauma systems and CT availability have modified its role. Current international guidelines primarily recommend FAST in patients who cannot immediately undergo CT because of:
The examination was later expanded to include assessment of both pleural cavities for pneumothorax and hemothorax, giving rise to the Extended FAST (E-FAST) protocol.
crumbs: 13  Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol

title: Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol
crumbs: 13  Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol

title: Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol
section: What can be identified by the FAST protocol?
text:
What can be identified by the FAST protocol?

Diagnostic performance
Recent systematic reviews reported the following diagnostic performances:

Diagnostic performance of FAST and E-FAST. Adapted from (4). *More recent studies have reported higher sensitivities.


Finding
Sensitivity (95% CI)
Specificity (95% CI)




Abdominal free fluid
74% (73–76%)
98% (97–98%)


Pericardial effusion
91% (87–94%)
94% (92–96%)


Pneumothorax (E-FAST)
69% (66–73%)*
99% (98–99%)



The consistently high specificity indicates that a positive FAST examination strongly suggests clinically significant injury. Conversely, its moderate sensitivity means that a negative examination cannot reliably exclude traumatic injury.


A negative FAST does not exclude severe trauma
FAST is designed to detect free fluid, not to identify every traumatic injury. A normal examination does not exclude:

solid organ injuries without significant hemoperitoneum;
bowel or mesenteric injuries;
pancreatic injuries;
retroperitoneal hemorrhage;
diaphragmatic rupture;
vascular injuries.

FAST should therefore not be considered a comprehensive abdominal examination.






ImportantGolden rules of FAST




FAST is primarily a rule-in examination. A positive study strongly supports hemoperitoneum or cardiac tamponade and may justify immediate intervention in haemodynamically unstable patients.
A normal FAST examination never excludes significant abdominal injury. When clinical suspicion persists, further evaluation—including CT when feasible—remains necessary.





Role of FAST in trauma management








Clinical situation
FAST result
Suggested management




Haemodynamically unstable
Positive
Immediate hemorrhage control according to local resources (surgery or interventional radiology)


Haemodynamically unstable
Negative
Continue evaluation, repeat FAST if indicated, and search for another cause of shock


Haemodynamically stable
Positive
Consider CT to characterize injuries and guide treatment


Haemodynamically stable
Negative
FAST does not exclude injury; CT should be performed if clinically indicated









NoteKey messages




FAST is a focused examination developed to rapidly detect life-threatening internal bleeding.
Its greatest value is in haemodynamically unstable trauma patients.
A positive FAST strongly supports hemoperitoneum or cardiac tamponade and justifies immediate treatment without other imaging for unstable patients
A negative FAST never excludes severe injury.
crumbs: 13  Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol

title: Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol
text:
References




1. Emergency Physicians AC of. Ultrasound guidelines: Emergency, point-of-care and clinical ultrasound guidelines in medicine. Annals of Emergency Medicine. 2017 May;69(5):e27–54. doi:10.1016/j.annemergmed.2016.08.457


2. Grace S. Rozycki, M. Gage Ochsner, Jonathan H. Jaffin, Howard R. Champion. Prospective evaluation of surgeons’ use of ultrasound in the evaluation of trauma patients. Journal of Trauma: Injury, Infection & Critical Care. 1993 Apr;34:516–27.


3. Bell RM, Krantz BE, Weigelt JA. ATLS: A foundation for trauma training. Annals of Emergency Medicine. 1999 Aug;34(2):233–7. doi:10.1016/S0196-0644(99)70238-6


4. Diagnostic accuracy of eFAST in the trauma patient: A systematic review and meta-analysis [Internet]. Vol. 21. 21. doi:10.1017/cem.2019.381
crumbs: 13  Ultrasound for Abdominal Trauma: the FAST Protocol

title: How to Perform a FAST Examination
text: FAST examination overview
crumbs: 14  How to Perform a FAST Examination

title: How to Perform a FAST Examination
text:
FAST views
The FAST protocol consists of four standard views:










View
Plane
Probe position
Probe marker
Main structures visualised




Right Upper Quadrant (RUQ)
Coronal (frontal)
Right posterior axillary line, through the lower intercostal spaces
Towards the patient’s head
Liver, right kidney, hepatorenal recess (Morison pouch), diaphragm, pleural space, spine


Left Upper Quadrant (LUQ)
Coronal (frontal)
Left posterior axillary line, through the lower intercostal spaces
Towards the patient’s head
Spleen, left kidney, splenorenal recess, diaphragm, pleural space, spine


Suprapubic
Transverse
Midline, immediately above the pubic symphysis
Towards the patient’s right
Bladder, rectovesical/rectouterine pouch, uterus or prostate


Subxiphoid
Transverse
Immediately inferior to the xiphoid process, directed towards the left shoulder
Towards the patient’s right
Liver, pericardium, four cardiac chambers






Four views of the FAST examination. AI-generated



Tip
The suprapubic examination should ideally include both transverse and sagittal views, as this improves the sensitivity for detecting small volumes of pelvic free fluid.



Which probe to choose?
The FAST exam visualises deep structures and requires a low frequency probe. Literature is heterogeneous for choice between abdominal and sectorial probes, and both may be used in practice.
crumbs: 14  How to Perform a FAST Examination

title: How to Perform a FAST Examination
text:
Right Upper Quadrant (Morison pouch)
The RUQ view evaluates the hepatorenal recess (Morison pouch) and the right paracolic gutter.

Image Acquisition
Place the probe on the right posterior axillary line, through the lowest intercostal spaces, with the probe marker pointing towards the patient’s head.
A satisfactory image should simultaneously include:

liver
right kidney
hepatorenal interface (Morison pouch)
diaphragm
spine




Normal right upper quadrant view. From The POCUS Atlas




Troubleshooting

If the probe is too superior, aerated lung obscures the image.
If the probe is too anterior, bowel gas limits visualization.
Slide posteriorly until both the liver and kidney appear in the same image.
Ask the patient to take a deep inspiration, if possible, to bring the liver below the rib cage.



Interpretation
Look for anechoic (black) free fluid within:

the hepatorenal recess (Morison pouch)
the inferior tip of Morison pouch
the right paracolic gutter




Positive RUQ. From The POCUS Atlas
crumbs: 14  How to Perform a FAST Examination

title: How to Perform a FAST Examination
text:
Left Upper Quadrant (splenorenal recess)
The LUQ view evaluates the splenorenal recess and the left paracolic gutter
Compared with the RUQ, this view is usually more technically challenging because the spleen is smaller and positioned more posteriorly and superiorly.

Image Acquisition
Place the probe on the left posterior axillary line through the lowest intercostal spaces with the probe marker directed towards the patient’s head.
A satisfactory image should include:

spleen
left kidney
splenorenal recess
diaphragm
spine extending above the diaphragm




Normal right upper quadrant view. From




Troubleshooting

Position the probe slightly more posterior and cephalad than on the right side.
Scan through the intercostal spaces.
Ask the patient to inhale deeply whenever possible.



Interpretation
Search for anechoic free fluid in:

the splenorenal recess
around the inferior pole of the spleen
the left paracolic gutter




Normal right upper quadrant view. From





Positive LUQ (subdiaphragmatic free fluid). From The POCUS Atlas
crumbs: 14  How to Perform a FAST Examination

title: How to Perform a FAST Examination
text:
Suprapubic views (Douglas pouch)
The pelvic view evaluates the Douglas pouch. Both transverse and sagittal views should ideally be obtained.

Transverse view

Image Acquisition
Place the probe immediately above the pubic symphysis with the probe marker pointing towards the patient’s right.
The bladder should be used as an acoustic window.
The examination should visualise:

Male: bladder, rectovesical pouch (Douglas), rectum
Female: bladder, uterus, rectouterine pouch (Douglas), rectum

Sweep the probe from the bladder dome towards its base using a short-axis sliding or tilting motion.



Normal transversal suprapubic view (male). From The POCUS Atlas




Troubleshooting

Direct the probe slightly posteriorly if the bladder is difficult to visualise.
A partially filled bladder is usually sufficient.
Gas within the rectum may obscure posterior structures.
The seminal vesicles may resemble fluid, but unlike free fluid they have smooth, symmetric margins and do not conform to surrounding anatomy.



Interpretation
Look for free fluid:

between the bladder and rectum in males
between the uterus and rectum in females




Positive suprapubic transversal view (male). From The POCUS Atlas





Positive suprapubic transversal view (female). From The POCUS Atlas





Sagittal view
Rotate the probe 90° clockwise so that the probe marker points towards the patient’s head.
Sweep from one side of the bladder to the other while maintaining the bladder in the centre of the image.
The sagittal view improves the detection of small fluid collections and better demonstrates the uterus and rectouterine pouch.



Normal suprapubic sagittal view (male). From The POCUS Atlas





Positive suprapubic sagittal view (male). From The POCUS Atlas
crumbs: 14  How to Perform a FAST Examination

title: How to Perform a FAST Examination
text:
Subxiphoid cardiac view
The subxiphoid view evaluates the pericardial space for hemopericardium.

Image Acquisition
Place the probe immediately inferior to the xiphoid process with the probe marker directed towards the patient’s right. Aim the ultrasound beam towards the patient’s left shoulder.
Use the liver as an acoustic window.
The image should include:

liver
right ventricle
left ventricle
right atrium
left atrium
pericardium

Note that right cavities are closer from the probe than left cavities.
If gastric gas limits the view, apply gentle pressure towards the spine rather than towards the xiphoid process. Pressure on the xyphoid process is painful and must be avoided.



Normal subxyphoid view. From The POCUS Atlas




Troubleshooting

Increase posterior angulation towards the heart.
Ask the patient to take a deep inspiration if clinically feasible.
Slide slightly to the patient’s right to maximise the liver acoustic window, if spine trauma is not suspected.



Interpretation
Look for anechoic fluid surrounding the heart.
The presence of a pericardial effusion does not by itself indicate cardiac tamponade and requires additional findings, such as right-sided chamber collapse and clinical features.
In an unstable trauma patient, a large pericardial effusion (> 1 cm) without other obvious cause of shock strongly suggests tamponade and should prompt immediate consideration of pericardial drainage.



Positive subcostal view (mild effusion). From The POCUS Atlas





Positive subcostal view (large effusion). From The POCUS Atlas
crumbs: 14  How to Perform a FAST Examination

title: Heart - Learning Objectives
text:
TipLearning objectives



After completing this chapter, learners will be able to:

Explain the indications and limitations of point-of-care ultrasound cardiac examination.
Obtain all standard transthoracic views
Recognize normal anatomy in each view.
Identify:

mild and severe alteration left ventricle ejection fraction using visual semi-quantitative assessment, EPSS and MAPSE
pericardial effusion and consequences on right ventricle
increased intravascular volume using vena cava diameter and respiratory variation
signs of acute cor pulmonare

Interpret findings according to the clinical context.
Recognize common pitfalls and avoid frequent mistakes.

Note that the use of doppler imaging (including colour, pulsed, continuous and tissue) is out of the scope of this chapter.

title: Standard cardiac ultrasound views
text:
Improving image acquisition
Focused transthoracic cardiac ultrasound relies on four standard views:
The inferior vena cava (IVC) is usually assessed from the subcostal window using a longitudinal view.

title: Standard cardiac ultrasound views
text:
Probe selection
The preferred probe for cardiac ultrasound is the sectorial (or phased array) probe. If unavailable, another low-frequency probe (such as the curvilinear probe) can be considered although image quality may be reduced.


Patient position
Whenever possible (notably if no spinal trauma is suspected), position the patient in the left lateral decubitus position, which brings the heart closer to the chest wall and often improves image quality.

title: Standard cardiac ultrasound views
text:
Parasternal long axis (PLAX) view

Image Acquisition
The probe is placed immediately lateral to the left edge of the sternum, in the highest intercostal space allowing for visualisation. The probe marker must point towards the right shoulder.
An adequate PLAX view should demonstrate:

aortic valve opening
mitral valve opening
left ventricle in long axis
left atrium
right ventricle (infudibulum)
proximal ascending aorta
thoracic descending aorta




Normal Parasternal Long Axis (PLAX) view. From thepocusatlas.org




Troubleshooting
Note that the PLAX may be challenging and is not possible on all patients. Four parameters of probe position and orientation must be considered to improve view:

Find the highest intercostal space allowing for visualisation of the heart. Usually, it is between the second and fifth intercostal space
Use short-axis tilting motion to find the angle allowing for visualisation of both mitral and aortic valves
Use long-axis tilting or sliding motion to center the aortic and mitral valves on the center of the screen
Rotate the probe until the left ventricle is viewed in his longest axis

title: Standard cardiac ultrasound views
text:
Parasternal Short Axis (PSAX) view
The Parasternal Short Axis (PSAX) view is usually obtained at three different segments: basal, median, and apical. This allows for assessment of the contractility of all segments of the left and right ventricles.

Image acquisition
From the PLAX position, rotate the probe 90° clockwise, such that the probe marker points toward the left shoulder. Tilt the probe to orient the ultrasound plane perpendicular to the heart axis. Correct orientation of the ultrasound plane is confirmed when the left ventricle is shaped as a regular circle.
From this, use a short-axis slide motion along the heart long axis, to obtain short axis views at three levels:

basal level, confirmed by visualising the mitral valve
mid-ventricular level, confirmed by visualising the papillary muscles
apical level

This allows for assessment of the segmentar wall motion.



Normal Parasternal Long Axis (PLAX) view. From thepocusatlas.org




Troubleshooting

Ensure the left ventricle is always centered. Use long axis sliding or tilting to correct if needed.
An elliptical heart view suggests that the ultrasound plane is not perpendicular to the long axis of the heart. This is corrected by short-axis tilting, or rotating.

title: Standard cardiac ultrasound views
text:
Apical four chambers (A4C) view
The apical view provides a comprehensive assessment of cardiac chamber size and global ventricular function.

Image acquisition
The following sequence helps for identifying the apical view:

From the PSAX view, slide down to the apex of the heart in short axis. The probe marker points towards the left of the patient
Slide the probe one intercostal space below
Tilt the probe to orient the ultrasound beam towards the long axis of the heart
Adjust the view with sliding and tilting motions to ensure the interventricular septum is aligned with the centre of the image, and both the mitral and tricuspid valves are clearly visualised




Normal Apical Four Chambers (A4C) view. From thepocusatlas.org




Troubleshooting

The apex of the heart may be difficult to localise. Use short axis views, or palpate the point of maximal impulse
Lung interposition may obscure the cardiac apex. Position the patient on the left side if possible
The ultrasound plane must be adjusted with tilting and rotating movements to ensure that the ultrasound plane passes through the center of both mitral and tricuspid valves
Sometimes, correct visualisation of the right ventricle is not possible and only the left ventricle is fully visualised

title: Standard cardiac ultrasound views
text:
Subcostal (subxyphoid, SX) transversal view

Image acquisition

Position the probe in transversal view, just inferior to the xyphoid processus, with the probe marker pointing towards the left of the patient
Lower the probe handle toward the abdominal wall to direct the ultrasound beam toward the heart.




Normal subcostal (SX) view. From thepocusatlas.org




Troubleshooting

Be careful with the pressure exerced by the probe, especially on the xyphoid which can be very painful
Asking the patient to relax the abdominal muscles, bending the knees or inhaling deeply may help visualising the heart

Interposition of gastric gases may hide the subcostal view. In such case, use a long-axis sliding motion towards the right of the patient, to use the liver as an ultrasound window

title: Standard cardiac ultrasound views
text:
Inferior vena cava long-axis view

Image acquisition

Position the probe in a transversal view through the epigastric fossa, the probe marker pointing towards the left of the patient
Centre the IVC on the screen
rotate the probe counterclockwise while maintaining the IVC in the center of the screen




Longitudinal IVC view (dilated). From thepocusatlas.org




Troubleshooting

The rotation movement must be performed slowly, without loosing visualisation of the IVC during the maneuver
The IVC must be seen entering the right atrium, tto avoid confusing it with the aorta

title: Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction
text:
Quantitative and semi-quantitative assessment methods of the LVEF
Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF) can be measured:
While quantitative assessment is preferred in cardiology consultation (follow-up, decision for treatment, …), the semi-quantitative method is preferred in emergency and ICU settings (lang_recommendations_2015?).
crumbs: 17  Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction

title: Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction
text:
quantitatively, estimating a value
semi-quantitatively, estimating a category (usually three: normal, mild alteration, severe alteration)



Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction methods








Method
Type
Measurement
Use and remarks




Teicholtz
Quantitative
Infers the LVEF from the shortening fraction between basal anteroseptal and posterior walls
Obsolete: assumes normal LV geometry


Simpson biplane
Quantitative
Measures two perpendicular areas of the LVEF in systole and diastole
Gold standard method for quantitative assessment


Visual assessment
Semi-quantitative
Visual assessment of LV contractility
Preferred method in Emergency and ICU


EPSS
Semi-quantitative
Minimum distance between the anterior leaflet of the mitral valve and the septum in systole (M-mode, PLAX)
Useful adjunct to visual assessment


MAPSE
Semi-quantitative
Travel distance of the lateral mitral annulus between diastole and systole (M-mode, A4C view)
Useful adjunct to visual assessment


Mitral Annular Speed (S’)
Semi-quantitative
Peak speed of the anterior movement of the mitral annulus (TDI mode, A4C)
Requires Tissue Doppler Imaging (TDI) mode









Important



In this course focused on emergency settings, we will develop only:

Visual assessment
Adjunct indices: EPSS, MAPSE
crumbs: 17  Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction

title: Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction
text:
Visual assessment of the LVEF
LVEF can be visually assessed in all standard views.

PLAXPSAXA4CSX








Normal PLAX. From thepocusatlas.org







Severely altered LVEF, PLAX. From thepocusatlas.org













Normal PSAX. From thepocusatlas.org







Mild alteration of LVEF, PSAX. From thepocusatlas.org







Severely altered LVEF, PSAX. From thepocusatlas.org













Normal A4C. From thepocusatlas.org







Severely altered LVEF, A4C. From thepocusatlas.org













Normal subcostal view. From thepocusatlas.org







Severely altered LVEF, SX. From thepocusatlas.org
crumbs: 17  Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction

title: Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction
text:
Indices

E-Point Septal Separation (EPSS)
The E-Point Septal Separation (EPSS) is the measure of the distance between the anterior mitral valve leaflet and the septum, during the early phase of the diastole. It is usually measured in parasternal long axis view (PLAX) in M-mode, with the motion line placed at the height of the tip of the mitral valve leaflet.



Abnormal EPSS. From thepocusatlas.org


The usual threshold values are:

EPSS > 7 mm has a 100% sensitivity and 51.6% specificity for LVEF below 30% (mckaigney_e-point_2014?).
EPSS > 10 is usually considered as highly specific of severe alteration of LVEF, although the evidence is weaker (ginzton1985?), (moynihan1981?).



Mitral Annular Plane Systolic Excursion (MAPSE)
The Mitral Annular Plane Systolic Excursion (MAPSE) is the measure of the distance traveled by the lateral mitral valve annulus during systole. It is a reflect of the longitudinal component of the systolic function. It is measured in M-mode in four-chamber view.



Measure of the MAPSE. From (cirin2024?)


Usual thresholds are:

MAPSE > 10 mm highly suggestive of normal LVEF
MAPSE < 8 mm highly suggestive of altered LVEF
crumbs: 17  Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction

title: Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction
text:
Summary

Semi-quantitative multimodal assessment of the LVEF








Measure
Normal (> 50%)
Mild alteration (35-50%)
Severe alteration (<35%)




Visual contractility
Normal
Mild motion reduction
Severe motion reduction


EPSS
< 7 mm
7-10 mm
> 10 mm


MAPSE
> 10 mm
8-10 mm
< 8 mm (6 mm for some authors)
crumbs: 17  Assessing the Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction

title: Inferior Vena Cava
text: Measure of Inferior Vena Cava Diameter and Collapsibility

title: Inferior Vena Cava
text:
Recommended measure method
Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) diameter is a reflect of the cardiac filling pressures. It is recommended in the guidelines to measure the IVC diameter (mukherjee2025?):

In transversal view, inner diameter
1–2 cm distal (caudal) to the junction of the hepatic veins with the IVC
Measure both largest diameters in expiration and inspiration

Current recommended thresholds are:

IVC < 21 mm with collapsibility > 50%: RAP 3 mmHg
IVC > 21 mm with collapsibility < 50%: RAP >15 mmHg




Collapsed IVC (longitudinal view). From thepocusatlas.org





Enlarged IVC (longitudinal view). From thepocusatlas.org





Collapsed IVC (transversal view). From thepocusatlas.org




Pitfalls

Between inspiration and expiration, the IVC may shift sideways, resulting in suboptimal diameter measure in longitudinal view. In such cases, measuring the anteroposterior diameter of the IVC in transversal view may reduce the risk of error
Mechanical ventilation induces increased intrathoracic pressure, resulting in dilated IVC whereas the intracardiac filling pressures are decreased

title: Inferior Vena Cava
section: How does IVC relate to fluid responsiveness?
text:
How does IVC relate to fluid responsiveness?
Interpretation of the IVC to diagnose hypervolemia is mostly consensual. More controversial is its reliability to predict fluid responsiveness. Indeed, IVC diameter and respiratory variation is not considered a reliable stand-alone predictor of fluid responsiveness (orso2020?), (kim2021?), especially in spontaneously breathing patients (cardozojúnior2023?).

title: Acute Cor Pulmonale
text:
Ultrasound features of Acute Cor Pulmonale
Acute Core Pulmonale (ACP) is defined as an acute alteration in the structure and function of the right ventricle (RV) of the heart caused by a primary disorder of the respiratory system, resulting in pulmonary hypertension (garrison2023?). Its first cause is massive pulmonary embolism (PE), although only massive PEs cause ACP.
Other causes of ACP include:
Features of Acute Cor Pulmonale must be differentiated from Chronic Cor Pulmonale, which can be caused by all causes of chronic pulmonary hypertension. Note that in those cases, the right ventricle may be dilated, but is usually also thicker (consecutive to compensatory hypertrophy).

title: Acute Cor Pulmonale
text:
Right ventricular dilatation
The dilatation of the right ventricle is best identified by comparison of the diameter between left and right ventricle. The ratio between right and left diastolic diamaters (RV/LV ratio) is measured as:

freezing the image at end-diastole, where the ventricular dimensions are the largest
measure the maximal internal diameter of right and left ventricles, from inner edge to inner edge, at same level, perpendicular to the long axis, approximately one-third of the distance from the tricuspid annulus to the apex.
Compute \(\textrm{RV/LV}_{\textrm{ratio}} = \frac{\textrm{RV}_{\textrm{diameter}}}{\textrm{LV}_{\textrm{diameter}}}\)

The recommended thresholds are:

A RV/LV > 0.6 suggests a moderate RV dilatation
A RV/LV > 1 suggests a severe RV dilatation (arrigo2024?)




Dilated right ventricle with intracardiac thrombus. From thepocusatlas.org




Right ventricular systolic dysfunction
The systolic function of the right ventricle is usually estimated visually in emergency setting. Similar to the MAPSE, the Tricuspid Annular Plane Systolic Excursion (TAPSE) can be measured. Usual thresholds are (altinsoy2025?):

TAPSE < 17 mm: normal
TAPSE 15-16 mm: mild alteration
TAPSE 13-14 mm: moderate alteration
TAPSE < 13 mm: severe alteration




TAPSE measurement. From (mukherjee2025?)




Paradoxical Septal Wall Motion
When the pressure increases in the right ventricle, the septum wall is pushed from the right ventricle towards the left ventricle, changing the shape of the right ventricle from a circle to a “D” shape.



Paradoxical septal wall motion. From thepocusatlas.org




Dilated Inferior Vena Cava
A diameter of the VCI > 21 mm measured in transversal view 2 cm below the supra-hepatic vein, or a respiratory variation of < 50% suggests increased IVC pressure (arrigo2024?).


McConnell’s Sign
McConnell’s sign is defined as a normal RV apical motion, while the motion of the median free wall of the right ventricle is impaired.



Mc Connell’s sign. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Acute Cor Pulmonale
text:
Synthesis



Acute Cor Pulmonale features. From (arrigo2024?).

title: Pericardial Effusion
text: Identifying a Pericardial Effusion

title: Pericardial Effusion
text:
Typical characteristics of pericardial effusion
Pericardial effusion manifests as an anechoic collection peripheral to the heart. It can be:

Circumferential
or localised

It can be observed in any heart view.

Parasternal Long AxisParasternal Short AxisApical Four ChambersSubcostal





Pericardial effusion, PLAX view. From thepocusatlas.org







Pericardial effusion, PSAX view. From thepocusatlas.org







Pericardial effusion, A4C view. From thepocusatlas.org







Pericardial effusion, SX view. From thepocusatlas.org







Differential diagnosis

Epicardial fat pad
Small effusions may be difficult to identify and distinguish from epicardial fat pad.

Differential diagnosis between pericardial effusion and epicardial fat pad.







Feature
Pericardial effusion
Epicardial fat pad




Echogenicity
Anechoic or nearly anechoic
More echogenic, heterogeneous, often with internal speckes


Movement
Fluid remains stationary while the heart moves with it
Moves synchronously with the myocardium


Typical location
Circumferential or dependent; often posterior when small
Most commonly anterior, especially over the right ventricle


Shape
Smooth fluid layer conforming to the pericardial space
Irregular or lobulated appearance


Dynamic appearance
Maintains an echo-gree space throughout the cardiac cycle
Thickness and contour change as the myocardium contracts






Fat pad and pericardial effusion. From thepocusatlas.org




Left pleural effusion
Left pleural effusion can mimick a pericardial effusion. The best distinctive feature is the presence of fluid between the left atrium and the descending aorta, confirming the pericardial effusion. In pleural effusions, the fluid stays lateral to the descending aorta.



Pericardial effusion. Note the fluid between the left atrium and the descending aorta. From thepocusatlas.org





Left pleural effusion. Note that the effusion does not fuse between the left atrium and the descending aorta. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Pericardial Effusion
text:
Importance and hemodynamic consequences
Once a pericardial effusion has been identified, the assessment aims at:

estimating the importance of the effusion
evaluating the consequences on cardiac function


Importance of the effusion
The importance of the effusion is measured in end of diastole. ESC 2025 guidelines classify pericardial effusion as:

Pericardial effusion size classification. From (schulz-menger2025?).


Effusion size
Classification




< 10 mm
Mild


10-20 mm
Moderate


> 20 mm
Large



Importantly, the importance of the effusion is poorly related to the consequences on the heart: although effusions inferior to 10 mm are not considered as significant, mild or moderate effusions can cause tamponade whereas important effusions can be asymptomatic.






Important



Volume of the effusion is poorly correlated with severity.




Cardiac consequences
The following signs suggest significant hemodynamic compromise, characteristic of tamponade:

Right Atrium Diastolic Collapse (RADC)
early Right Ventricular Diastolic Collapse (RVDC)
IVC dilatation




Pericardial effusion with right atrium diastolic collapse (RADC) and right ventricle diastolic collapse (RVDC). From thepocusatlas.org





Pericardial effusion with right ventricle diastolic collapse (RVDC). From thepocusatlas.org

title: Lungs - Learning Objectives
text:
TipLearning objectives



After completing this chapter, learners will be able to:

Explain the indications and limitations of point-of-care ultrasound lung examination.
Identify and interpret typical lung ultrasound signs and artifacts including:

A-lines
B-lines
Lung sliding
Lung pulse
Lung point
Spine sign
Lung condensation

Identify features of common pulmonary conditions including

Pneumothorax
Pleural effusion
Interstitial edema, including hydrostatic edema and pneumonia

title: Standard Lung Views
text:
Examination areas
Three areas of explorations are considered in lung ultrasound:
Modern protocols suggest exploring 12 areas for comprehensive ultrasound lung assessment.

title: Standard Lung Views
text:
the highest point, where pneumothorax is visible if present,
the lowest point (pleural recessus), where effusion is visible is absent
lung parenchyma, to look for signs of edema





12 lung areas. From (1)

title: Standard Lung Views
text:
Choice of the probe
The choice of the probe for lung ultrasound has been debated. Recent publications suggest that:

Linear, high-frequency probes improve visualisation of the pleura motion (lung sliding)
Low-frequency probes (sectorial, curvilinear) allow for visualisation of pleural effusion and may increase visualisation of B-lines

title: Standard Lung Views
text:
Main views

Intercostal spaces
Intercostal spaces must be visualised with the probe mark pointing upwards, towards the head of the patient. There, the pleura is visualised between two ribs and their acoustic shadows:



Normal ultrasound view of an intercostal space. From thepocusatlas.org




Pleural recessus
Pleural recessus are visualised by placing the probe laterally, near the costal insertion of the diaphragm, with the probe mark poiting upwards. The position is very similar to the RUQ and LUQ in the FAST protocol, but slightly superior. The view must visualise:

The diaphragm
The liver (right) or the spleen (left)
The spine

The antero-inferior portion of the lung may interpose in front of the probe during inspiration, hiding the structures beneath it. This is described as the “curtain sign”.



Normal ultrasound view of the right pleural recessus, with curtain sign in inspiration. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Standard Lung Views
text:
References




1. Brenner DS, Liu GY, Omron R, Tang O, Garibaldi BT, Fong TC. Diagnostic accuracy of lung ultrasound for SARS-CoV-2: A retrospective cohort study. The Ultrasound Journal. 2021 Dec;13(1):12. doi:10.1186/s13089-021-00217-7

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text:
A-lines
In lung ultrasound, the strong reflection caused by water/air interfaces produces various artifacts, which are as essential as anatomical findings for exam interpretation.
A-lines appear as horizontal lines (perpendicular to the ultrasound beam), at regular intervals. They are caused by almost total reflection of the ultrasound pulses on the pleura (see Artifacts section for details).
A-lines do not have neither pathological nor physiological meaning. They can be present or absent under any pathology, and should not be interpreted.

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text: A-lines. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text:
B-lines
B-lines appear as vertical lines (aligned with the ultrasound beam). They are caused by small amounts of water right under the visceral pleura. Therefore:

they indicate subpleural edema if 3 or more are present at one exam site
they can be caused either by hydrostatic edema or inflammatory edema from pneumonia. Almost differential diagnosis between those two conditions cannot rely on ultrasound only, the following patterns are frequent:

hydrostatic edema results in symetric and declive B-lines pattern, with more B-lines on bottom than on top of lungs. They are frequently associated with pleural effusion.
pneumonia results in assymetric, focal B-lines pattern (although multifocal or diffuse pneumonia can cause B-lines at multiple sites). They may be associated with pleural effusion, although less frequent.

in case of pneumothorax at the point of exam, B-lines can’t be seen as the ultrasound pulses are reflected by the air contained in the pleural cavity. Therefore, B-lines rule out pneumothorax.




B-lines. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text:
Spine sign
When examining the pleura recessus through the liver (on the right) or spleen (on the left), the thoracic spine is normally not visible as the ultrasound beam is reflected over the diaphragm, by the air contained in the lungs. In case of pleural effusion, the air is replaced by liquid, allowing transmission of the ultrasound beam to the spine, making it visible.
Therefore, visualisation of the thoracic spine, named “spine sign”, rules in pleural effusion.



Spine sign. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text:
Lung consolidation
Lung consolidation manifests as “abnormally visible lung”, i.e. visualisation of the lung parenchyma, which is normally invisible because of the air in it reflecting all ultrasounds. Visualisation of the lung parenchyma therefore suggests loss of lung aeration, making lung parenchyma visible.
Lung consolidation can be caused by:

atelectasia, secondary to infection or other causes
passive consolidation caused by a pleural effusion




Lung consolidation with effusion (left) over the liver and diaphragm (right). From thepocusatlas.org

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text:
Lung sliding
In absence of pneumothorax, the parietal and visceral layers of the pleura are in contact, and the motion of the visceral pleura is visible. This is called the “lung sliding”.
In contrast, in presence of pneumothorax, the ultrasound beam is reflected by the air contained in the pleural cavity, before reaching the visceral pleura. The loss of lung sliding therefore suggests pneumothorax, although other causes exist (superficial ventilation, auto-PEEP, pleural adherence…).



Lung sliding present. From thepocusatlas.org





Lung sliding absent. From thepocusatlas.org








Important




Presence of lung sliding rules out pneumothorax at the site of examination
Absence of lung sliding does not rule in pneumothorax



Note that as pleural air moves upwards, the search for lung sliding must be done on the highest point of the thorax (apical for seated patients, mid-thoracic for patients in complete decubitus).
Some authors recommend the use of M-mode to improve visualisation of the lung sliding:

Presence of lung sliding will show speckles on the infra-pleura space of the image, whereas the intercostal muscles (supra-pleural area) appear as horizontal lines. This is described as the “seashore sign”
Absence of lung sliding will manifest as horizontal lignes at both supra-pleural and infra-pleural areas. This is described as the “barcode sign”




Left: lung sliding present, showing the “seashore sign”. Right: lung sliding absent, showing the “barcode sign”. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text:
Lung pulse
The lung pulse is a variant of lung sliding where the heartbeat motion is transmitted to the visceral layer of the pleura. It has the same interpretation than the lung sliding.






Important




Presence of lung pulse rules out pneumothorax
Absence of lung pulse does not rule in pneumothorax

title: Lung Ultrasound Semeiology
text:
Lung point
The lung point is described as the transit of the point of separation between the visceral and parietal layers of the pleura under the ultrasound beam. This causes a dual image where:

lung sliding is seen on the the side of the pleural window where the two layers are in contact (“before” the pneumothorax)
lung sliding is absent on the the side of the pleural window where the two layers are separated (“after” the pneumothorax)

Lung point is the only sign ruling in pneumothorax.






Important




Presence of lung point rules in pneumothorax
Absence of lung point does not rule out pneumothorax






Lung point. From thepocusatlas.org

title: Pathological features
text:
B-lines in pulmonary edema are usually approximately symetric and declive, whether assymetric focal in pneumonia.↩︎
Pleural effusion is more frequent in pulmonary edema than in pneumonia, and is usually approximately symetrical in pulmonary edema, and assymetrical in pneumonia.↩︎

text:
Test text.

According to (volpicelli2012?) …
Pleural sliding excludes pneumothorax at that location.1
See Figure 26.1.

text: Not necessarily the entire hemithorax.↩︎
